## Definition

Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles containing an outer *mitochondrial membrane* and an *inner mitochondrial membrane*, separated by a small fluid-filled intermembrane space. The large, fluid-filled cavity enclosed by the inner membrane is known as the *mitochondrial matrix*.

## Core Principles

- The inner membrane is folded into tubules called *cristae*, which substantially increase the surface area for the electron transport chain.
- Mitochondria contain their own special DNA (which we inherit from our mothers), which can make 13 proteins that are needed to make other important proteins for the mitochondria.
- Similar to the nucleus, mitochondria possess their own genetic material, paralleling the nucleus in housing DNA within the organelle.
- Because mitochondria can also make their own proteins, ribosomes (i.e. the protein-making factories) are present in the mitochondrial matrix.

## Sub-concepts

- **Outer mitochondrial membrane** — the outer boundary of the organelle.
- **Inner mitochondrial membrane** — folded into *cristae* to expand surface area for the electron transport chain.
- **Intermembrane space** — small fluid-filled space between outer and inner membranes.
- **Mitochondrial matrix** — large fluid-filled cavity enclosed by the inner membrane; houses mitochondrial DNA and ribosomes.
- **Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA)** — maternally inherited; encodes 13 proteins; analogous to nuclear DNA in being contained within the organelle.
- **Mitochondrial ribosomes** — enable in-organelle protein synthesis.
- **Cristae** — tubular folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane that expand surface area for the electron transport chain.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- The expanded inner-membrane surface area provided by cristae supports the electron transport chain, the basis of aerobic ATP production relevant to sport and exercise biochemistry.
- Maternal inheritance of mtDNA underlies clinical and physiological discussions of mitochondrial inheritance patterns.
